reasoning: All four correctly decompose gravity into five jobs and keep the sports on separate axes, so the contest is execution, surprise, and fertility. D wins: WICK reframes "falling" as a portable wandering "down" you collectively create and chase (the freshest take on that mechanic), SUTURE turns deleted parabolas into a hardening cat's-cradle of frozen light-rays that is the most beautiful and most generative single idea in the set, and KEEL bravely makes the vestibular system the entire field while honestly owning the spectator risk; craft is vivid and consistent throughout. A is a close second: SUMP (opaque mass-bluffing, "poker with conservation of momentum") is the single boldest idea anywhere, and SHROUD's socially-constructed ground is conceptually daring, but PAROLE's timed crosswind jets and SHROUD's contested sheet are slightly fiddlier and one notch less elegant than D's cleanest ideas. C ranks third on the strength of PARALLAX (loss of a shared "down" as a perceptual, frame-corrupting sport) and the sharp "winning makes you fragile" beat in BALLAST, but QUORUM reuses the collective-balance idea and CAROM is a fairly standard billiards-room. B is last and safest: a rotating-shell field and a ricochet-billiards throw are the more predictable solutions, with lower boldness and originality despite competent prose.
